Eden Lane Condominium Association · Hanover Twp., NJ Prepared April 25, 2026

The Eden Lane Landscape Brief

A board-level review of six BrightView proposals totaling roughly $100,400 — what works, what needs fixing, and what we should ask for before we sign.

ToThe Board & Manda Perrone
ReSix BrightView Proposals
Total$100,433.61 baseline
DecisionRevisions before signing
I · The bottom line first

The plant choices are sound. The privacy plan is not.

BrightView's plant selections are appropriate and the pricing is in line with the NJ commercial market. But the road-facing screen along Eden Lane — our most visible, most expensive frontage — is specified as a slow grow-in plan that won't deliver real privacy for two to three years. With targeted revisions, we can fix this for an additional $8,000–$18,000 and get day-one screening that's also more disease-resistant, more deer-resistant, and better suited to the salt and pollution exposure along a public road.

i
Eden Lane needs heavy screening

The road frontage with retaining walls is currently exposed. Privacy from the road is the highest-priority outcome.

ii
Pool area should keep the sun

Trees too tall too close to the pool will block the summer sun residents want. Screening here must use shorter species or be carefully positioned.

iii
Roadside plants face salt and exhaust

Anything along Eden Lane must tolerate winter de-icing salt spray and vehicle pollution. Several proposed species don't.

iv
This is an expensive, long-lived investment

Disease and pest resistance matter. Replacing dead $400 evergreens in 2028 because we picked deer candy in 2026 is the failure case to avoid.

II · The site today

Three areas, three different problems.

Most of these proposals address one of three distinct challenges: an exposed road-facing slope along Eden Lane where mature trees were recently removed, the Boxwood Court parking islands and building frontage where shade canopy was lost to active stump grinding, and the smaller interior island and terrace work. The photos make this clear.

Eden Lane · road frontage and pool

Buildings sit above multi-tier retaining walls. Sightlines from Eden Lane run up the slope into the property — and the apartment building across the road has clear sightlines back into the pool area. Privacy here is the highest priority.

Boxwood Court · parking islands and building frontage

Where mature trees came down — fifteen tree locations plus four additional stumps. Residents lost shade canopy, screening between buildings, and aesthetic uniformity. This is where Proposal #5 ($24,225) goes, and active stump grinding work is already underway.

IV · The Eden Lane fix

What the proposal delivers vs. what we actually need.

As Proposed

Grow-in plan, $14,487
  • 6 × Norway Spruce at 6–7 ft (gaps between them)
  • 4 × Red Maple at 2–2.5" caliper (deciduous, sparse)
  • 7 × Weigela at 3-gal (knee-high)
  • 7 × Spirea at 3-gal (knee-high)
  • Wildflower mix on the slope (seasonal only)
  • No on-center spacing specified
  • No plant warranty written in

Result: visible gaps for 2–3 years; minimal winter screening; pretty in 2029.

→

Recommended Revision

Day-one privacy, $20K–$24K
  • 10–12 × Green Giant Arborvitae at 10–12 ft, staggered double row, 6–8 ft on center
  • 2 × Red Maple moved to pool-facing side for fall color
  • Mid-layer shrubs upgraded to 7-gal minimum
  • 5–7 × Inkberry Holly along road edge (salt & pollution tolerant evergreen)
  • Wildflower mix only on pool-facing slope
  • Spacing specified; 80%+ visual block at 3, 6, 9 ft heights at install
  • 1-year plant warranty

Result: real privacy starting opening weekend, salt-tolerant, doesn't shade pool.

The four-layer approach

A privacy screen is a structural problem, not a planting problem. The road-side observer sees three height bands: knee, eye, and building. Each band needs material doing screening work — or the gap shows.

i
Tall evergreen wall (back)

Green Giant Arborvitae or Nellie Stevens Holly. Year-round, dense, fast-growing. Staggered double row along the back of the berm.

10–25 ft
ii
Mid-height flowering shrubs

Limelight Hydrangea, larger Spirea, Viburnum, or Ninebark in 7-gallon size. Fills the eye-level gap, adds seasonal color.

4–7 ft
iii
Salt-tolerant front layer

Inkberry Holly and Northern Bayberry along the road edge. Both handle road salt and exhaust well. Inkberry stays evergreen — winter screening at the curb.

3–6 ft
iv
Decorative deciduous (pool side only)

The 4 Red Maples relocated to the pool-facing side of the berm. Pretty fall color for residents; doesn't compete with road-side screen; doesn't shade the pool.

10–25 ft

VI · Decision plants in detail

The eight plants that drive the screening decision.

These are the species that matter most for the Eden Lane privacy outcome and the Boxwood Court canopy restoration. Each card shows the resistance profile, the role in the design, and a placeholder for a real photograph that you'll add before publishing or sharing.

Adding photos before publishing

Each card below has a labeled placeholder showing the filename to use (e.g., green_giant_arborvitae.jpg). To add real photos:

  1. Save photos with the exact filenames shown into a photos/ folder next to this HTML file
  2. Replace each placeholder block with: <img src="photos/FILENAME.jpg" alt="...">
  3. Add credit caption underneath each photo (suggested format: "Photo: [Source/Photographer], [Year]")

Recommended free/credited photo sources: Monrovia, Plant Addicts, Nature Hills, Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder, USDA NRCS PLANTS Database, Wikimedia Commons (filter by license).

VII · Disease, deer, and pest summary

The three things that kill expensive plantings.

1. Bagworms — the #1 risk to evergreens in our plan

Bagworms are common in NJ and they specifically target arborvitae and spruce — the backbone of every screening proposal. Heavy infestation can defoliate evergreens in a single season. There's no preventative spray; treatment requires inspection and Bt application after eggs hatch in late May/early June.

Mitigation: Add an annual bagworm inspection and treatment line item to our ongoing maintenance contract. Cost is modest (typically $200–$500/year for a property our size). Loss avoidance is significant — replacing a single mature 12-ft arborvitae costs $700–$1,000.

2. Deer — winter browse on arborvitae

Hanover Township has deer pressure. Standard arborvitae (Thuja occidentalis) is one of the most deer-favored evergreens in winter — they strip lower branches when other food is scarce, leaving permanent bare zones at the base. Mitigation: Substitute Green Giant Arborvitae (markedly more deer-resistant) for the road-side screen, and Nellie Stevens Holly (spiny leaves, deer avoid) for the pool side. Deer spray treatments through winter are an option but ongoing cost.

3. Disease — needlecast on spruce, blight on boxwood

Norway Spruce in NJ is increasingly affected by needlecast diseases (Rhizosphaera, Stigmina) that cause needle drop and thinning. Boxwood blight is now established statewide; Rutgers issued NJ-wide alerts as recently as March 2026. Mitigation: Diversify the evergreen mix (don't plant a monoculture of any one species), and confirm BrightView's drip irrigation on the boxwood terrace is set up correctly to keep water off boxwood foliage.

4. Salt and exhaust — the slow killer

Plants closest to Eden Lane will be hit by winter road salt spray. Several species in the proposals (Norway Spruce, Weigela, Spirea) have only moderate salt tolerance — they'll survive but may show damage on the road-facing side. Inkberry Holly, Northern Bayberry, and Eastern Redcedar all have proven high salt tolerance and are explicitly listed in NJ DOT roadside planting guides.

VIII · Is the labor pricing fair?

Reverse-engineering the labor portion of each proposal.

BrightView bundles labor into the planting line items rather than breaking out hours, but we can estimate fairness by working backward from market labor rates and comparing to the totals.

Market benchmarks

Based on April 2026 NJ data: landscape laborers earn $17.68–$23.68/hour (NJ average ~$20/hr). Two-person commercial crews bill at $50–$100/hour all-in. Industry standard is roughly 80% of total project cost going to labor + overhead, with materials at 20% — though for plant-heavy installs, materials run higher (30–50%) because plants themselves are expensive.

Proposal Total Est. Material Cost Implied Labor Crew-Days Verdict
Eden Lane Berm (#3) $14,487 $5,500–$7,000 $7,500–$9,000 ~5–7 days Fair
Building 29 / Main Entrance (#1) $25,997 $10,000–$12,000 $14,000–$16,000 ~10–14 days Fair, but get retaining wall broken out
Boxwood Court Tree Replacement (#5) $24,225 $13,000–$15,000 $9,000–$11,000 ~7–10 days Fair (15 trees + stumps)
Center Islands (#6) $22,215 $11,000–$13,000 $9,000–$11,000 ~7–10 days Fair (6 islands)
Pool Area Screen (#2) $7,893 $3,500–$4,500 $3,500–$4,500 ~2–3 days Fair
Boxwood Terrace Irrigation (#4) $5,617 $1,500–$2,000 $3,500–$4,000 ~2 days Slightly high — irrigation is specialty work

Overall labor verdict: in line with NJ commercial market. BrightView is not the cheapest contractor available — a small local landscaper might come in 10–20% lower — but as a national commercial firm they carry full insurance, have crews that reliably show up, and can warranty the work. The premium is reasonable for an HOA context where liability and reliability matter.

Where labor pricing might be slightly elevated: the irrigation upgrade (#4) comes out at roughly $2,500/day for what's mostly trenching and assembly — that's at the high end. Worth asking if any of the soaker hose footage can be reduced if not all 1,400 feet are needed.

Where labor pricing is well-justified: the Eden Lane berm and Building 29 jobs both involve significant grading work, retaining wall labor, and large-tree handling — that's where commercial crew pricing earns its keep. Don't try to negotiate down on these.

What to verify before signing

Ask BrightView for a materials list with per-unit pricing on the two largest proposals (#1 and #5). This isn't always granted but it lets us see if any single plant is being marked up disproportionately. As a sanity check: a 10-12 ft Green Giant Arborvitae installed should be $580–$1,000 each at NJ commercial pricing in 2026.

IX · Per-proposal recommendations

What to ask for, proposal by proposal.

Eden Lane Berm Major Revision

Proposal #3 · "Area in front of pool" · SO# 8896331
Current $14,487

Required Changes

  • Substitute the 6 Norway Spruce with 10–12 Green Giant Arborvitae at 10–12 ft (or upsized Norway Spruce if Green Giant unavailable)
  • Plant in a staggered double row, 6–8 ft on center along the road-facing length
  • Move the 4 Red Maples to the pool-facing side of the berm
  • Add 5–7 Inkberry Holly + 3–5 Northern Bayberry along the road edge for salt tolerance
  • Upgrade Weigela and Spirea from 3-gal to 7-gal minimum, or substitute 3-4 Limelight Hydrangea (7-gal)
  • Reduce wildflower mix to pool-facing slope only
  • Specify in writing: "80% visual block at 3 ft, 6 ft, and 9 ft heights at time of install"
  • Add 1-year plant warranty

Estimated revised cost: $20K–$24K (+$5K–$10K over current).

Building 29 / Main Entrance Screen Revise & Clarify

Proposal #1 · SO# 8899686
Current $25,997

Required Changes

  • Confirm location: if visible from Eden Lane, apply same upsizing as Eden Lane berm
  • Specify all 24 "Standard - Shrub/Perennial 1 gal" line items by species and cultivar
  • Break out the retaining wall re-lay as a separate line with its own cost
  • Substitute 2–3 of the 9 Arborvitae for Green Giant Arborvitae for diversity
  • Add 1-year plant warranty

Boxwood Court Tree Replacement Approve with Edits

Proposal #5 · SO# 8872477
Current $24,225

Stump grinding in progress at Building 38 — site prep for the 15 replacement trees.

This proposal is well-designed. Diversified species selection (Red Maple, Hornbeam, Zelkova, Hackberry) is excellent for shade canopy and resilient to disease. The active stump grinding work confirms BrightView is already executing on site prep. Minor cleanup only.

Required Changes

  • Clean up the "7 ?? Ilex glabra" typo and confirm cultivar
  • Specify the Hackberry cultivar ('Prairie Sentinel' for columnar)
  • Add 15–25 understory shrubs (Hydrangea arborescens, Itea, or Clethra)
  • Confirm staking and mulch rings included
  • Verify scope: confirm whether the marked-for-removal parking island tree (cones + paint markings) is included in this SO or billed separately
  • Add 1-year plant warranty

Center Islands Approve with Edits

Proposal #6 · SO# 8855323
Current $22,215

Required Changes

  • Specify all "Standard - Shrub/Perennial" line items by species (11 on Island 700, 15 on Island 1700)
  • Confirm the 2 Zelkovas on Island 200 fit the island's mature footprint
  • Add 1-year plant warranty

Pool Area Screen Approve as-is

Proposal #2 · SO# 8896358
Current $7,893

Smallest job and the only one with a written 1-year plant warranty already included. Consider asking BrightView to substitute Nellie Stevens Holly for the 7 Arborvitae if pool-side deer pressure is a concern; otherwise approve as written.

Boxwood Terrace Irrigation Approve as-is

Proposal #4 · SO# 8878793
Current $5,617

Drip line + soaker hose is exactly the right approach for boxwood blight prevention. Confirm BrightView sanitizes tools between properties and ask whether 1,400 feet of soaker hose is necessary for the actual terrace area.

X · The bottom line

What we're really deciding

The choice isn't whether BrightView's plan is "good" — it is. The choice is whether we want a plan that looks pretty in three years, or a plan that delivers privacy this summer and survives the next decade of NJ winters, deer, bagworms, and road salt. The difference is roughly $8,000–$18,000 spread across one major and several minor revisions.

Current Total $100,434

Six proposals as written. Sound but underbuilt for stated priorities.

Estimated Revised Total $108K–$118K

With privacy upgrades, salt-tolerant additions, warranties, and species cleanup.

Value of the Delta Day 1

The added cost buys immediate privacy along Eden Lane, plus disease/deer/pest resilience.

XI · Action items

Six things to do before signing.

  1. Confirm photo-to-proposal mapping. Request a site map from BrightView showing exactly which SO# covers which physical location.
  2. Confirm the pool/road orientation — does the pool face north or south of Eden Lane? Determines whether 12 ft Green Giant or 8 ft Nellie Stevens Holly is right for the road-side berm.
  3. Send revision request to BrightView via Manda — focusing on Proposal #3 (Eden Lane berm) as the priority.
  4. Get a competing bid from one local NJ landscaper on the two largest proposals (#1 and #5) just to confirm BrightView's pricing.
  5. Add bagworm IPM to the ongoing maintenance contract — annual inspection plus Bt spray as needed.
  6. Request itemized materials list with per-unit plant pricing on the two largest proposals (#1 and #5).
— Eden Lane Landscape Brief —
Prepared April 25, 2026 · For board discussion before sign-off
